coredata.dc:title: Thermal control of oxygen-induced emission states in carbon dots for indoor lighting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Byun, Yerim
Jung, Chan-Woo
Kim, Ji-Hee
Kwon, Woosung
coredata.dc:description: Understanding the role of heteroatoms in carbon dots (CDs) has been recognized as a critical factor for the design and engineering of their optical properties. Oxygen is one of the most prevalent heteroatom defects in CDs; however, its effects on the optical properties are still unclear because it has not been possible to precisely control the degree of oxidation of CDs. Here, we report the synthesis of CDs in a controlled process that allows thermal control of the oxygen content using benzyl alcohol as an oxidizing solvent. The results suggest that oxygen-induced defects reduce the optical band gap of CDs, and their emission is red-shifted from blue (428 nm) to red (628 nm). The photoexcited charge-carrier dynamics of the CDs were thoroughly studied using transient absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence decay measurements. Furthermore, the bright multicolored emission of our CDs renders them suitable for sun-like panchromatic indoor lighting applications. We believe that these new insights into oxygen-induced defects in CDs will result in significant progress in their practical use.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Oxygen
Defects
Fluorescence
Lighting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and morphological characterization of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the formation of HODs from BzOH and the synthesis of CDs from CA with HODs.

TEM images of (a) CD-230, (b) CD-250, (c) CD-280, and (d) CD-300. The scale bars represent 10 nm. XRD patterns of (e) CD-230, (f) CD-250, (g) CD-280, and (h) CD-300. The deconvoluted spectra 2θ1,2 and 2θ3 are associated with d002 and d101, respectively.
C 1s XPS spectra of (a) CD-230, (b) CD-250, (c) CD-280, and (d) CD-300. O 1s XPS spectra of (e) CD-230, (f) CD-250, (g) CD-280, and (h) CD-300.

ABS (black) and FLE (colored) spectra of (a) CD-230, (b) CD-250, (c) CD-280, and (d) CD-300. FL contour maps of (e) CD-230, (f) CD-250, (g) CD-280, and (h) CD-300. EX and EM stand for excitation and emission, respectively. The grid lines (gray) are guides for the eye.

FL quenching spectra of (a) CD-230, (b) CD-250, (c) CD-280, and (d) CD-300 for various acetic acid concentrations. Stern–Volmer plots for FL quenching of (e) CD-230, (f) CD-250, (g) CD-280, and (h) CD-300. The grid lines (gray) are guides for the eye.

Normalized TRPL decay curve of CDs probed at emission (EM) wavelengths of (a) 430 nm, (b) 550 nm, and (c) 630 nm. TA kinetics of CDs at probe wavelengths (λ probe) of (d) 430 nm, (e) 550 nm, and (f) 630 nm. (g) Schematic illustration of electronic transitions associated with OID states.
(a) Photograph of phosphor film containing CD-300 under daylight. (b) Structure, (c) photograph, and (d) FL spectrum of white LED fabricated by combining phosphor films under optimal conditions.
Summary of the photophysical properties and rates of the CDs.

head.ce:title: Thermal control of oxygen-induced emission states in carbon dots for indoor lighting applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Materials and Systems, Sookmyung Women's University, Seoul, 04310, South Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Sookmyung Women's University, Seoul, 04310, South Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Energy Science, Sungkyunkwan University, Suwon 16419, South Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Understanding the role of heteroatoms in carbon dots (CDs) has been recognized as a critical factor for the design and engineering of their optical properties. Oxygen is one of the most prevalent heteroatom defects in CDs; however, its effects on the optical properties are still unclear because it has not been possible to precisely control the degree of oxidation of CDs. Here, we report the synthesis of CDs in a controlled process that allows thermal control of the oxygen content using benzyl alcohol as an oxidizing solvent. The results suggest that oxygen-induced defects reduce the optical band gap of CDs, and their emission is red-shifted from blue (428 nm) to red (628 nm). The photoexcited charge-carrier dynamics of the CDs were thoroughly studied using transient absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence decay measurements. Furthermore, the bright multicolored emission of our CDs renders them suitable for sun-like panchromatic indoor lighting applications. We believe that these new insights into oxygen-induced defects in CDs will result in significant progress in their practical use.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles have unique mechanical, optical, magnetic, and catalytic properties depending on their size and chemical composition. Many studies have investigated the effects of size, heteroatom incorporation (i.e., chemical doping), and surface modification [1–3]. It has been demonstrated that the optical properties of semiconductor nanocrystals (quantum dots) can be widely tuned by varying their composition and size-controlled quantum confinement.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of luminescent carbon nanomaterials with quasi-spherical morphology and a characteristic size of less than 10 nm [4,5]. CDs have attracted increasing attention for both fundamental and practical research owing to their attractive properties, particularly their fluorescence (FL), bioavailability, and thermochemical stability [6,7]. Consequently, they have been applied in various areas including biomedicine [8], light-emitting devices [9,10], photochemical sensors [11,12], data security [13,14], photovoltaics [15,16], and photocatalysis [17,18]. Furthermore, improvements in the functionality and diversity of CDs and products containing them have recently been accelerated by the development of simple and efficient methods for their preparation and functionalization [19,20].

The chemical structure of CDs is complex and still not completely known; however, the core is typically described as partially graphitic or polycrystalline with a mixture of graphitic and polymeric structures, and the surface is thought to be covered with heteroatom-containing functional groups and chemical defects [21,22]. Although they have various structures and compositions, CDs share several optical properties: (i) a large Stokes shift [23], (ii) excitation-dependent [24] or even multichromatic fluorescence [25], and (iii) a wavelength-dependent FL lifetime [26]. The contributions of certain physical/chemical structures in CDs to these optical properties are unclear [27,28]; however, incorporating heteroatoms into CDs by doping has been found to considerably enhance the color purity and emission intensity of their FL [29,30].

An understanding of the roles of heteroatoms in CDs is considered critical for designing a robust synthetic scheme and ultimately tailoring the optical properties for specific applications. Heteroatoms with lone electron pairs, such as nitrogen and oxygen, are particularly interesting because they can easily form strong covalent bonds with carbon and generate defect states (or intragap states) [31]. Previous studies have demonstrated that the effect of heteroatoms on the optical band gap of CDs depends strongly on the chemical structure of the precursors and the chemical states of the heteroatoms in CDs [32–34]. Ding et al. synthesized CDs with tunable FL from blue to red, which was attributed to surface oxidation [35]. Wang et al. reported a facile method for the preparation of CDs with full-spectrum emission, which was attributed to the combined effects of particle size and sp2-hybridized carbon [36]. Ding et al. designed a solvothermal method for CDs with tunable FL ranging from 443 nm (blue) to 745 nm (red), which was ascribed to the synergistic effects of the p-electron system, graphitic nitrogen, and surface states [37]. These studies offer valuable insights into the FL mechanism and highlight the potential of CDs for practical applications. However, most previous research has focused on nitrogen because it can significantly change the optical properties of CDs. In contrast to nitrogen, oxygen has generally been regarded as a negative defect that degrades the optical properties, especially the FL color purity. Moreover, the effects of oxygen on CDs have rarely been investigated because the precise control of the oxygen content or the degree of oxidation in CDs is still limited (Table S1, Supplementary Material) [29].

In this paper, we present a new approach to tailoring the optical band gap of CDs by controlling the degree of oxidation using citric acid (CA) as a carbonization source and benzyl alcohol (BzOH) as an oxidizing solvent. The FL color (wavelength) of the CDs can be varied from blue (428 nm) to red (628 nm) by varying the reaction temperature, which affects the oxidizing power of BzOH. The chemical origin of the FL of the CDs was systemically investigated using Stern–Volmer quenching analysis. The correlation between the oxygen-induced structural/chemical changes and carrier dynamics of the CDs was thoroughly studied using pump-probe spectroscopy. Finally, we demonstrated the practical utility of the CDs by fabricating sun-like white light-emitting devices (LEDs) with freestanding and flexible phosphor films based on CDs, which may replace toxic or costly rare-earth phosphors such as yttrium, lanthanide, europium, and terbium.

CA (anhydrous, 99.5%), BzOH (anhydrous, 99.8%), toluene (anhydrous, 99.8%), and acetone (anhydrous, 99.5%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) was purchased from Microchem. All chemicals were used as received, without further purification or modification.

The CDs were prepared as follows. CA (2660 mg) was soaked in BzOH (9 mL), and the mixture was vigorously stirred under ambient conditions for 24 h. After a predetermined time, the mixture was transferred to a stainless-steel hydrothermal autoclave and heated to 230, 250, 280, or 300 °C for approximately 9 h. The samples are referred to as CD-230, CD-250, CD-280, and CD-300, respectively. The obtained sticky product was then redispersed in toluene and dialyzed against toluene for at least three days using a dialysis bag (Spectra/Por Biotech Cellulose Ester Dialysis Membranes, Spectrum Chemical) with a cut-off molecular weight of 100–500 Da. After the toluene was removed by rotary evaporation, the samples were redispersed in acetone (1 mg/mL) and stored in a argon-purged desiccator until measurements were performed. The reaction yields of CD-230, CD-250, CD-280, and CD-300 were 5.00, 27.28, 29.82, and 49.66, respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a JEOL JEM-2200FS instrument equipped with a Cs corrector. Dynamic light scattering measurements were performed using a Horiba SZ-100 particle-size analyzer. Raman spectroscopy was performed on an Alvatek XperRam S spectrometer with 532 nm laser excitation. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a VG Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with an Al X-ray source (1486.6 eV). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer equipped with an attenuated total reflection accessory.

For all spectroscopic measurements, 10 × 10 mm2 QS-grade quartz cuvettes (111-QS, Hellma Analytics) were used. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Scinco S-3100 spectrophotometer. FL spectra were recorded using a Jasco FP-8500 fluorometer. The absolute quantum yields (QYs) were measured using a Jasco ILF835 integrating sphere (100 mm) and the Jasco Spectra Manager II Version 2 software. For transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy, a 1 kHz Ti:sapphire amplifier (Legend Elite, Coherent) with a central wavelength of 790 nm and a pulse duration of approximately 30 fs was divided into two beams. One beam was sent to an optical parametric amplifier (TOPAS Prime, Light Conversion) to generate a pump pulse, which was tuned to 350 nm with a pulse energy of 100 nJ/pulse. The other beam was focused on a nonlinear crystal as a probe beam to generate a visible white light continuum. The probe beam monitored the pump-induced absorption changes as a function of the time delay between the pump and probe pulses. TA data were acquired using a TA spectrometer with a 1024-pixel CMOS sensor (HELIOS, Ultrafast Systems). The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) was measured using a spectrometer combined with a streak camera (C10910, Hamamatsu). The excitation wavelength was 350 nm, and the pulse energy was 100 nJ/pulse.

A 5 mL aliquot of a suspension of the CDs in acetone (2 mg/mL) was mixed with 5 mL of a 10 wt% solution of PMMA. The mixture was vortexed for 10 min, and an aliquot was drop-cast onto a cleaned glass substrate (1 mL per 10 cm2). The coated glass substrate was left overnight on a flat table under ambient conditions for solvent evaporation. The obtained films were peeled from the substrate and placed over a UV (375 nm) light-emitting diode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and morphological characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of the CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Briefly, with increasing synthesis temperature, more BzOH was oxidized and converted into highly oxidative derivatives (HODs) such as benzaldehyde and benzoic acid [38]. These HODs participated in the carbonization reaction to form oxygen-induced defects (OIDs) in the CDs. Because OIDs are known to play an important role in the optical responses of CDs, they are expected to control the FL of the CDs depending on temperature. A detailed description of the protocol is provided in the experimental section.

The morphology and size of the as-prepared CDs were examined by TEM (Fig. 2 a–d and Fig. S1). The CDs were quasi-spherical. Fig. S2 shows that the average size of the CDs increased slightly from 3.97 nm (CD-230) to 4.76 nm (CD-300) with increasing temperature because the carbonization reaction causing the growth of the CDs is promoted at higher temperatures. The reaction yield of the CDs increased significantly, from approximately 5.0% (CD-230) to 50% (CD-300) with increasing temperature (Table S2). The Raman spectra of the CDs (Fig. S3) exhibit a typical signal of partially graphitic carbon as a mixture of sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon [39]. Specifically, all of the CDs exhibited two distinct peaks at ∼1350.15 and ∼1588.75 cm−1, which were associated with the D and G bands, respectively, of partially graphitic carbon [40]. The D/G ratio was calculated to be ∼0.975 irrespective of the synthesis temperature (Table S3), indicating that the CDs might contain a significant amount of oxygen or OIDs.

The polycrystalline nature of the CDs was further confirmed by XRD analysis (Fig. 2e–h). The strong, sharp carbon (002) diffraction band (2θ = 15–30°) indicates that the CDs were composed mainly of disordered graphitic (or turbostatic) carbon structures [41], and the relatively weak and broad carbon (101) diffraction band (2θ = 40–50°) indicates the presence of graphitic carbon in a specific region [42]. These spectra were further convoluted to simulate the limited experimental resolution. The interplanar distances of the carbon (002) (d002) and (101) (d101) planes were calculated to be approximately 4.4–5.0 (2θ1 ∼ 17–20° and 2θ2 ∼ 26–27°) and 2.0–2.2 Å (2θ3 ∼ 40–45°), respectively (Table S4). According to these data, we conclude that the structure of the CDs consists of a highly oxidized carbon network with embedded graphitic subregions [43,44].

XPS was used to analyze the chemical structure of the CDs. The C 1s spectra of all the CDs (Fig. 3 a–d) show the characteristic C−C/CC (284.8 eV) peak attributed to the carbonization of CA. The O 1s spectra (Fig. 3e–h) show the carbonyl CO (533 eV) and C−O (532 eV) peaks, which can be attributed to oxygen-containing defects in the CDs originating from the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups of CA and BzOH. The elemental analysis results in Table 1 show that the oxygen content of the CDs increased with temperature, from 5.13% (CD-230) to 11.55% (CD-300). The reason is that the thermal conversion of BzOH into HODs was promoted at higher temperatures, and thus more OIDs were formed in the CDs. This result is consistent with the XRD data in Table S4, which show that d002 and d101 increased slightly with temperature, implying that more oxygen atoms were incorporated into the carbon network and may have increased the (average) interstructural distance between graphitic subregions in the CDs [45,46].

FT-IR spectroscopy was performed to examine the surface functional groups of the CDs. As shown in Fig. S4, the FT-IR spectra of all the CDs show peaks for alcohol O–H stretching (3200 and 2700 cm−1), aromatic C–H bending (1938 cm−1), aldehyde CO stretching (1732 cm−1), and cyclic alkene CC stretching (1597 cm−1), indicating that their surface chemical structures were almost identical regardless of the synthesis temperature. Interestingly, the aldehyde CO stretching peak tended to split into two peaks at higher synthesis temperatures. This peak splitting is thought to be associated with the formation of conjugated unsaturated CO groups in small aromatic compounds [47]. Therefore, it can be explained by the promotion of the carbonization reaction at higher temperatures to form more aromatic compounds and develop graphitic microstructures in the CDs. Furthermore, in agreement with the atomic percentage values in Table 1, these results confirmed that more carbonyl groups were formed at higher temperatures, which contributed to the increased oxygen content of the CDs.

Fig. 4 a–d shows the UV–vis absorption (ABS) and FL excitation (FLE) spectra of the CDs. The CDs exhibited a strong, sharp ABS peak in the deep UV (∼250 nm) region, regardless of the synthesis temperature. This deep-UV ABS peak can be assigned to the π → π* transition, which is generally observed in organic molecules with carbonyl groups [26]. By contrast, a relatively weak, broad ABS band at approximately 400 nm generally develops with increasing temperature, which is known to be associated with the n → π* transition resulting from the presence of heteroatoms with lone pairs (oxygen in this case) or OIDs [48]. In addition, the FLE spectra were red-shifted with increasing synthesis temperature, where the FLE peak wavelengths for CD-230, CD-250, CD-280, and CD-300 were 375, 425, 490, and 555 nm, respectively. These results indicate that the CDs were oxidized to a greater extent at higher temperatures (which is consistent with the XRD and XPS data); as a result, more OIDs were formed, and thus more nonbonding states appeared near the Fermi level. These phenomena also promote the n → π* transition and absorption in the visible region; consequently, the color of the CD solutions became darker with increasing temperature (Fig. S5).

The optical band gap of direct band gap semiconductors can be estimated from the Tauc plot, in which (αhν)2 is plotted against hν, where α, h, and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively [49–51]. As shown in Fig. S6, by extrapolating the linear fit to the intercept of the abscissa (the energy axis), the optical band gaps of CD-230, CD-250, CD-280, and CD-300 were estimated to be 4.576, 4.567, 4.564, and 4.114 eV, respectively. Tauc estimation might not reflect every possible electronic transition in CDs. However, we confirmed that the optical band gap of the CDs decreased with increasing synthesis temperature; consequently, more nonbonding states associated with OIDs were generated.

The FL emission contour maps in Fig. 4e–h shows that the peak FL wavelength was dramatically red-shifted with increasing synthesis temperature from 428 nm (CD-230) to 460 nm (CD-250) to 555 nm (CD-280) to 628 nm (CD-300). The broad FL band in the long-wavelength visible region (500–700 nm) clearly became more pronounced at higher synthesis temperatures. As a result, as shown in Fig. S7, the FL color of the CD solutions under excitation light near their FL peaks changed from blue (CD-230) to green (CD-250) to yellow (CD-280) to red (CD-300). This result, with the ABS and FLE data, can be explained by the fact that higher temperatures promote the formation of OIDs and the associated electronic states, or OID states, in the CDs. Because their chemical origin is the nonbonding orbitals generated by the oxygen atoms of the CDs, these OID states are located inside the band gap, reducing the band gap energy and shifting the FL to a longer wavelength. This red-shift was less pronounced when CA, an oxygen donor, was omitted from the synthesis procedure, indicating that the shift was caused by the incorporation of oxygen into the CDs (Fig. S8). As shown in Fig. S9, the full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the FL spectra generally increased with increasing synthesis temperature from 66 nm (CD-230) to 76 nm (CD-250) to 158 nm (CD-280) to 191 nm (CD-300). The reason is that OIDs with more diverse chemical structures (especially in higher oxidation states) are formed at higher temperatures and generate OID states with different energy levels.

To systemically investigate the origin of the FL of the CDs, we performed Stern–Volmer analysis. The Stern–Volmer relationship for ideal quenching is as follows:
I 0
I
= 1 + K
[ Q ]
where I 0 and I are the FL intensities in the absence and presence of a quencher, respectively; K is the Stern–Volmer constant, and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher. In this study, acetic acid was employed as a quencher because a coordinate bond can be formed between acidic H+ ions and oxygen atoms on the surface of the CDs. Because these oxygen atoms generate the nonbonding orbitals and associated OID states, as explained above, H+ ions might be expected to capture the electrons in the nonbonding orbitals (or the OID states) to effectively quench the FL of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 5 a–d, the FL intensity of all CDs decreased linearly when acetic acid was added (Table S5) [52], whereas the FL wavelength was unchanged. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5e–h, the relative FL intensity, I 0/I, was linearly correlated with the concentration of acetic acid [Q]. These results indicate that a nonfluorescent complex was formed between the H+ ions donated by acetic acid and the oxygen atoms of the CDs [53], which implies that the OID states play a central role in the occurrence of FL in the CDs.

The TRPL was measured to investigate the recombination dynamics of the OID states in the CDs. We monitored the photoluminescence at 430, 550, and 630 nm, which corresponds to the emission wavelengths of the OID states in the CDs synthesized at different temperatures (Fig. 6 a–c). All the samples were excited at 350 nm (3.54 eV). Similar decay profiles were obtained at different emission wavelengths, indicating that the emission pathways are not strongly affected by the density of OID states. The obtained decay dynamics of the CDs were fitted to biexponential functions; two decay processes were identified with a nonradiative recombination lifetime (τ NR) of 1.17–3.15 ns and a radiative recombination lifetime (τ R) of 5.7–8.52 ns (Fig. S10a-c and Table S6). Both τ R and τ NR for blue emission (430 nm) decreased as the temperature increased, whereas the values for green (550 nm) and red (630 nm) emission remained relatively constant. The reason is that at higher temperatures, the photoexcited carrier density of the higher OID states decreases, and most photoexcited carriers contribute more to the emission of the lower OID states, in good agreement with our FL results. Average lifetimes (τ avg) for emission at 430, 550, and 630 nm were observed, regardless of the synthesis temperature (Fig. S11), suggesting that the radiative pathways were not significantly affected by the synthesis temperature. The TRPL data provide evidence that, in CDs synthesized at higher temperatures, the increased number of OID states results in further band-gap narrowing and contributes to red emission. In addition, the carbonyl group is well known as the main factor that increases the ratio of nonradiative to radiative recombination. Indeed, the amplitude of nonradiative recombination was ∼2.6 times greater than that of radiative recombination (Fig. S10d-f and Table S6), and the recorded FL QYs of the CDs were approximately 15–40% depending on the excitation wavelength (Fig. S12).

We then performed TA spectroscopy to further investigate the fast photophysical processes of the OID states in CDs. The spectrally and temporally resolved TA map shows a broad photoinduced absorption band over the entire probe wavelength range (Fig. S13). The TA spectra are red-shifted with increasing temperature, demonstrating the formation of lower OID states. The TA kinetics following pump excitation at 350 nm (3.54 eV), which reflects the time-dependent carrier population in the corresponding energy states, were detected at probe wavelengths of 430, 550, and 630 nm (Fig. 6d–f). A global analysis of the decay kinetics for each CD at different probe wavelengths revealed that the kinetics relaxed in three steps, independent of the probe wavelength (Fig. S14a-c). The fitting parameters are summarized in Table S7. The first component (τ 1), which corresponds to trapping to shallow OID states, was 0.22–0.52 ps for CD-230 and increased with increasing synthesis temperature to 9.84 ps for CD-300. The second component (τ 2) is attributed to deep OID states, which decay within 9.07–11.1 ps for CD-230 and 96.4 ps for CD-300. The third component (τ 3) is related to OID-mediated emission, which decays in a few nanoseconds and is in good agreement with the nonradiative recombination lifetime (τ NR) shown in Fig. S10a-c. The average decay times (τ avg) for the kinetics at 430, 550, and 630 nm decreased as the synthesis temperature increased (Fig. S15). Moreover, a pump excitation wavelength of 550 nm (2.25 eV) was used to confirm the carrier dynamics of the OID states in the CDs (Fig. S16). At a probe wavelength of 630 nm, the TA kinetics is barely seen for CD-230 and CD-250, whereas the TA kinetics of CD-280 and CD-300 had a noticeable amplitude. Because the carriers were excited at a state below the S1 state of the CDs by 550 nm excitation, only the second and third decay components in the kinetics at 630 nm detection were observed. Overall, we confirmed that the synthesis temperature strongly affects the formation of the OID states in the CDs and thus identified photocarrier dynamics with a longer lifetime at higher temperatures. In Fig. 6g and Table 2 , we summarize all the photophysical processes in the CDs.

Finally, we demonstrated freestanding phosphor films employing the CDs (Fig. 7 a and Fig. S17). The CD-based phosphor films were fabricated by drop-casting a suspension of CDs in PMMA/toluene on a glass substrate. Fig. S18 shows that the FL spectra of the phosphor films were slightly red-shifted and broadened compared with those of the corresponding CD solutions because of the inner-filter effect arising from the reabsorption of emitted light in condensed matter [54]. The CDs were uniformly dispersed in the films and embedded in a transparent PMMA matrix; their FL spectra were almost uniform across the films (Fig. S18). We stacked all the phosphor films, positioned them over UV (375 nm) LEDs, and applied a forward-bias current of 50 mA (Fig. 7b); we obtained white light (Fig. 7c) with a peak wavelength of 430 nm (Fig. 7d). As shown in Fig. S19, the Commission Internationale d’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of the white LEDs, (0.3070, 0.3849), are similar to those of the CIE standard illuminant D65 or sunlight, (0.3128, 0.3290). This result indicates that the CD-based phosphor films are suitable for indoor lighting [55]. Furthermore, the phosphor films exhibited outstanding air stability after 60 d of storage under ambient conditions (Fig. S20). Thus, owing to their flexibility and stability, CD-based phosphor films can be used in commercial fluorescent lamps to replace toxic or expensive rare-earth phosphors such as yttrium, lanthanide, europium, and terbium.

We discovered oxygen-related emission states originating from OIDs in CDs obtained by using a new strategy. The degree of oxidation of the CDs was thermally controlled by varying the oxidizing power of BzOH, which is proportional to the reaction temperature. As the synthesis temperature increased from 200 to 300 °C, more BzOH was converted to HODs; in addition, more OIDs and associated emission states were formed in the CDs, and their FL wavelength was red-shifted by 200 nm, from 428 to 628 nm. Stern–Volmer analysis confirmed that the nonbonding orbitals of OIDs played a central role in the generation of emission states and the occurrence of FL in the CDs. These results, in combination with the TA spectroscopy and TRPL results, further suggested that the visible emission of the CDs originates from carrier trapping by oxygen impurities or OIDs. Finally, the CDs were applied as a freestanding phosphor film in an LED with a UV backlight and successfully produced sun-like white light suitable for indoor lighting applications. Thus, we expect that these insights into the emission states of CDs will enable a better understanding of the optical properties of CDs and expand their potential as eco-friendly and sustainable phosphors.

Yerim Byun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Chan-Woo Jung: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Ji-Hee Kim: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Woosung Kwon: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Basic Science Research Program (NRF-2022R1A2C4002403) and Nano Material Technology Development Program (2009–0082580) of the National Research Foundation of Korea. J.-H.K acknowledges financial support from the Institute for Basic Science of Korea (IBS-R011-D1).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110895.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of carbon dots and their applications
RSC Adv
